ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka. FOODON_03302114 (paneer food product) is used as the closest parent. The mint marinade and tandoor grilling preparation are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cheese, fresh (unripened) — used as closest parent for paneer-based grilled dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for paneer tikka or mint-marinated grilled paneer. A0CKP (fresh unripened cheese) is the closest defensible parent. Preparation method (tandoor grilling) and mint-herb marinade are not encoded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 refers to paneer as a substance. No specific code exists for the prepared tikka dish with mint marinade.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka consists of approximately 6 pieces of grilled paneer cubes, weighing around 200 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer, raw — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for Cheese, cottage, creamed (FDC ID 173417) for micronutrient cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides raw paneer macronutrient values (per 100 g): energy 321 kcal, protein 18.3 g, fat 25.1 g, carbohydrate 3.6 g. These values are adjusted upward slightly for the marinated and grilled preparation: protein and fat remain close to raw paneer values as the marinade (yoghurt, mint, spices) adds minimal macronutrients per 100 g of the final dish. Sodium is elevated due to salt in the marinade. Values represent the composite grilled dish per 100 g edible portion. Micronutrients for calcium, iron, and phosphorus are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central paneer/fresh cheese entries.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of paneer / fresh cheese — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on paneer (GI ~27) as the dominant ingredient
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka. The GI is estimated at 27 based on paneer (fresh Indian cheese), which is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database with a GI of approximately 27 (low GI). Paneer is the dominant macronutrient contributor. The mint-herb marinade and spices contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (27 × 8.4) / 100 ≈ 2.3 (serving size 200 g, carbohydrate 4.2 g per 100 g = 8.4 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade salt and spices
hypertension.guidance: Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and avoid additional salt or high-sodium accompaniments such as chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~27) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 27 and a glycemic load of only 2.3 per standard serving, Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka is a suitable protein-rich, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the saturated fat content (~12.8 g per 100 g) warrants moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from full-fat paneer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content (~12.8 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~68 mg per 100 g) from full-fat paneer are relevant for individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion control is advised and substitution with low-fat paneer may reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka is a popular North Indian vegetarian appetiser consisting of cubes of fresh Indian cottage cheese (paneer) marinated in a vibrant green sauce made from fresh mint (pudina), coriander, green chillies, yoghurt, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices such as cumin and garam masala. The marinated paneer is skewered and cooked in a traditional clay tandoor oven at high heat, imparting a characteristic smoky char on the exterior while keeping the interior soft and creamy. The dish has a bright herbal flavour profile dominated by fresh mint and coriander, with a mild heat from green chillies and a subtle tang from yoghurt and lemon juice. Texturally, the paneer is firm yet yielding, with lightly charred edges. Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka is commonly served as a starter or snack, accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. It is a staple on Indian restaurant menus globally, including in Singapore where it is served at tandoori-specialist restaurants. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish with a low glycemic index (~27), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The calcium content from paneer (~480 mg per 100 g) is nutritionally significant. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian. Clinically, the saturated fat and sodium content are the primary considerations for cardiovascular and hypertension management respectively.
common_user_queries: What is Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka?
Is paneer tikka good for diabetics?
What is hara paneer tikka made of?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN (2017) provides raw paneer macronutrient values which are the primary basis for this record. The dish-level nutrient values are adapted from raw paneer with reasonable adjustments for the marinade and grilling process. No direct nutrient analysis of Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka as a prepared dish was found in an approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate based on paneer GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) raw paneer data and represent a best estimate for the composite grilled dish. A direct laboratory analysis of the prepared dish would be required for high confidence. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paneer entry or the USDA cross-reference for fresh cheese at the level of confidence required. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paneer entry. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paneer entry. 5. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on paneer as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Pudina Hara Paneer Tikka was found. 6. Sodium of 420 mg per 100 g reflects the marinated and salted preparation; raw paneer sodium is lower (~10–20 mg per 100 g per ICMR-NIN). 7. The dish is served at Zaffron Tandoori, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source record). 8. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific prepared dish.

Set GI to 27 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database, paneer) with gi_category 'low' (≤55 threshold correctly applied).
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit notes.
Used only closest_parent match_type values for all three ontology systems with honest gap notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions including Type 2 Diabetes.
